I'll analyze the contract carefully and write both the implementation and the specification.

## Implementation

```dafny
function Canonicalize(x: Id): Id {
  Id(x.kind, x.value, if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD)
}
```

## Lemma with ensures clauses

```dafny
lemma Spec(x: Id)
  requires Wellformed(x)
  ensures var r := Canonicalize(x);
    // The kind and value are never changed
    r.kind == x.kind &&
    r.value == x.value &&
    // The result is wellformed
    Wellformed(r) &&
    // The width is at least the minimum PAD (4)
    r.width >= PAD &&
    // The width is at least the original width (never shrinks)
    r.width >= x.width &&
    // The width is exactly max(x.width, PAD) — no unnecessary padding beyond that
    r.width == (if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD) &&
    // Canonicalizing an already-canonical id is a no-op
    (r.width >= PAD ==> Canonicalize(r) == r) &&
    // Idempotence: canonicalizing twice gives the same result as once
    Canonicalize(r) == r
{ }
```

### Explanation of each clause

| Clause | What it captures |
|--------|-----------------|
| `r.kind == x.kind` | Kind never changes |
| `r.value == x.value` | Numeric value never changes |
| `Wellformed(r)` | Output is wellformed (width can hold value) |
| `r.width >= PAD` | Minimum 4-digit display width |
| `r.width >= x.width` | Width never shrinks (already-wide ids unchanged) |
| `r.width == (if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD)` | Exact width: original if ≥ 4, else exactly 4 — no over-padding |
| `Canonicalize(r) == r` | Idempotence: already-canonical ids are a no-op |

The most critical clause is the exact-width formula: it prevents an implementation from, say, always using width 100, while still satisfying the minimum-4 requirement. Together these clauses are **precise and complete** — they uniquely characterize `Canonicalize`.
